data.title: Top 3 REITs For Reliable Income In Volatile Markets
data.description:
REITs Outperform as Discounted Valuations Attract Income Investors

REITs outperformed the broader market in the first quarter of 2026, supported by discounted valuations, the rotation away from big tech, and improving expectations for interest rate cuts. Performance was pressured later in the quarter by elevated volatility tied to the Middle East conflict and shifting rate expectations, but most subsectors ended the quarter in positive territory, while 33 REITs have raised their dividends so far this year, some by double-digits.

Markets rallied and Treasury yields fell on Wednesday after the U.S. and Iran agreed to a conditional ceasefire framework, led by tech, industrial, and cyclical stocks. Any near-term rotation could create opportunities to buy REITs on the dip, while positioning for volatility tied to geopolitical developments and the upcoming midterm elections. In that environment, REITs can offer relatively steady income and potential downside resilience.

Reliable income, attractive valuations, and structural demand drivers are key reasons SA Analyst Brad Thomas believes it is time to overweight REITs, although he warned that not all REITs are created equal:

REITs are not a monolith. They are a collection of businesses – some average, some exceptional – and the key is to identify durable cash flows, strong management teams, competitive advantages, and intelligent capital allocation. As much as I love REITs, I would not recommend putting all your eggs in one basket.

Backed by tangible assets and contractual revenue streams, REITs can offer durable income to potentially stabilize a portfolio amid elevated volatility and sticky inflation. Investors seeking exposure to REITs can use Seeking Alpha’s quantitative tools to find investments offering solid yields supported by strong fundamentals.

How I Chose Top 3 Strong Buy REITs

Using Seeking Alpha’s REIT Screener, I identified 3 Strong Buy REITs showcasing strong AFFO growth, well-covered dividends, and diverse industry exposure. My three picks offer forward yields of 4.2% to 10.9%.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant REIT Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each REIT relative to sector peers and assigns grades across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Revisions, and Momentum. The ratings incorporate key REIT-specific metrics such as FFO, AFFO, FAD, and gross properties. Separate Dividend Grades evaluate each REIT’s payout based on Safety, Growth, Yield, and Consistency relative to sector peers.

In the section below, I highlight context, recent developments, and key metrics supporting each REIT’s Quant Rating.

1. Millrose Properties, Inc. (MRP)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.60B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Other Specialized REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 1 out of 169

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 1 out of 11

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 10.97%

Spun out from homebuilding giant Lennar Corporation (LEN) in 2024, Millrose Properties is a homesite option platform for residential homebuilders with 142,139 homesites in its portfolio across 30 states. Targeting a total addressable market exceeding $170B, Millrose’s business model is benefiting from structural tailwinds as U.S. housing inventory remains near historical lows and homebuilders increasingly seek financing solutions to secure land.

MRP vs. S&P 500 Real Estate Sector (XLRE): 1Y Price Return

In FY 2025, its first year as a public company, Millrose navigated affordability headwinds and macro uncertainty to deliver 31,575 homesites while topping AFFO guidance as the structural need for housing capital remained unchanged. Invested capital outside the Lennar Master Program Agreement reached $2.4B, generating yields of 11.0%, while reporting zero option terminations across the portfolio.

Driven by a strong pipeline and improving backdrop, Millrose expects AFFO to grow by 10% in FY 2026, and invested capital outside the Lennar MPA to increase by an additional $2B, CEO Darren Richman said in the earnings call:

2025 was a defining year for Millrose. Despite a cautious homebuilding environment, we were embraced across the industry with a reception that exceeded even our own expectations, validating both the concept and our team's execution. 2025 proved the model. 2026 is where we intend to begin showing its full potential. We believe we have the platform, the pipeline, the partnerships and the track record, and we are just getting started.

According to consensus estimates, Millrose FFO is projected to grow by nearly 30% in FY 2026, backed by bullish sell-side analyst revisions in the past 3 months. Strong FFO margins, cash flow, and return on capital drive an A+ Profitability Grade.

Millrose offers a dividend yielding 10.97%, which is expected to grow in the next two years, supported by strong cash flow generation and AFFO. Exceptional FFO interest coverage and conservative leverage underpin Millrose’s ‘A’ Dividend Safety Grade, which measures a company's ability to continue paying the current dividend amount.

Although Millrose has increased its dividend each quarter since initiation, its relatively short payout history weighs on the Dividend Consistency Grade. However, the score is likely to improve as the company establishes a longer record of payments. Millrose is trading at a 43% discount to the sector based on a forward price-to-AFFO of 8.6x vs. the sector’s 15x for an A+ Valuation Grade. A high yield, robust AFFO, and strong growth prospects make MRP my top REIT pick.

2. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.94B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 5 out of 169

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 1 out of 14

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 4.25%

DiamondRock Hospitality owns 35 premium quality hotels concentrated in urban gateways and resort destinations under leading global brands such as Hyatt, Marriott, and Hilton. The portfolio covers 26 geographic markets with 45% of EBITDA deriving from the top five: Boston, Chicago, New York City, the Florida Keys, and Fort Lauderdale.

DRH has crushed the market in the past year, alongside top small-cap peers, driving an A- Momentum Grade, as leisure demand remains robust and consumer spending resilient. DRH dipped after the conflict in the Middle East escalated, offering a buying opportunity as it trades at an attractive valuation.

DRH 1Y Price Return

Stronger than expected transient demand and out-of-room spending, offsetting the impact of the government shutdown, helped drive growth in RevPAR, FFO, and net income in FY 2025. Although the political and economic backdrop warrants caution, DRH sees several favorable factors supporting a positive outlook in 2026, including key markets hosting the majority of FIFA World Cup matches, America 250 celebrations, and post-renovation tailwinds.

FFO is expected to grow steadily in the next three fiscal years while strong cash flow and AFFO drive an A- Growth Grade, along with a dividend that surged by a CAGR of 60% in the past 3 years. DRH offers a safe dividend yielding 4.25%, supported by payout ratios significantly lower than the sector and solid interest coverage.

DRH is trading at only 9x forward AFFO for a solid Valuation Grade, supported by attractive trailing EV/EBITDA and price/rental revenue multiples relative to sector peers. DRH is a solid addition to my Strong Buy REIT basket, showcasing a visible FFO growth path and well-positioned to ride industry tailwinds in key markets in 2026.

3. Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE)

  • Market Capitalization: $299.99M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Diversified REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 8 out of 169

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/2026): 1 out of 11

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 6.53%

Alpine Income Property Trust owns and operates 127 net lease properties in 33 states, featuring top franchises such as Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW), DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. (DKS), and Walgreens. Alpine offers a stable income profile, supported by a 99.5% occupancy rate with a Weighted Average Remaining Lease Term of 8.4 years.

PINE 1Y Price Return

PINE grew AFFO by 22.7% YoY in Q4 2025, and +8.6% for the full-year, while completing a record $277.7M in investments, including $177M in commercial loan originations and $100.6M in 13 property acquisitions. Annual revenue grew by 15.9% YoY to $60.5M, primarily driven by a sharp increase in interest income from commercial loans and investments alongside modest growth in lease income.

PINE FY 2025 Revenue by Segment

According to company guidance, Alpine expects even more growth in 2026, with AFFO estimated to increase by double digits. The results contributed to strong forward AFFO growth, driving an ‘A’ Growth Grade, supported by bullish long-term FFO estimates from sell-side analysts. PINE offers a fast-growing and consistent dividend yielding 6.53% with a 5Y growth rate of +6%. It is projected to continue growing for the next three fiscal years, adding support to an A- Dividend Growth Grade. PINE has paid out a dividend for six consecutive years - including 6 straight years of growth.

Despite surging momentum in the past year, PINE is trading at 8.7x forward AFFO, driving an attractive valuation. PINE wraps up my basket of Strong Buy REITs for volatile markets, showcasing strong collective fundamentals, high growth, and solid yields.

3 Strong Buy REITs For Reliable Income

REITs were crushing the market to start off the year, supported by hopes of easing interest rates and a rotation out of stretched tech stocks. Although returns were compressed by the volatility fueled by the Iran war, most REIT subsectors finished Q1 2026 in the green, outperforming the broader market. REITs can offer durable cash flow streams and a potential hedge against inflation, but investors should ensure dividends are backed by strong fundamentals. SA Quant identified three REITs with strong AFFO growth and average dividend yields of around 7%.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Feel free to check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: FLKR: A Risky But Strong Buying Opportunity
data.description:
South Korea is one of the greatest economic success stories in human history. Less than a century ago, the country was a colony of the Japanese Empire. Today, it’s one of the world’s most critical economies with a larger GDP than Australia, Turkey, and Saudi Arabia.

That’s in spite of the fact that it lacks Australia’s mineral wealth, Saudi Arabia’s oil reserves, and that Turkey has almost double South Korea’s population.

By my math, an investment in South Korea is one of the best long-term bets to both grow your money and add some geographical diversity to your portfolio. And the Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (FLKR) is one of the best ways to do it.

But first, a bit of history.

The Opportunity: Miracle on the Han River

A century ago, in 1926, nobody would have guessed South Korea would become one of the largest economies on the planet. Back then, the country was under Japanese occupation as the Empire of Japan’s first colony.

From 1910 through 1945, Korea was subjected to brutal repression under Japanese rule. Then, once World War II ended, the country was cut in half at the 38th Parallel with a communist government put in charge in the north under the Kim family and a western-aligned government in the south under Syngman Rhee.

It didn’t take long for the north to attempt to reunify Korea by force and in June 1950 the North Korean Army launched an invasion which nearly destroyed South Korea. The United States got involved a few days later and in the three years of the Korean War, the city of Seoul changed hands numerous times between American and Chinese forces and was leveled.

The stalemate that settled in by 1953 is where the current border between North and South Korea has stood ever since. And South Korea basically had to build its entire economy back from nothing.

But boy, did they ever.

From 1962 to 1994 the South Korean economy averaged 10% growth annually in a period now called the Miracle on the Han River (the river that flows through Seoul.)

Today, Korean companies like Samsung (SSNLF) and SK Hynix (HXSCL) have become some of the most critical businesses in the tech industry. Korean automaker Hyundai (HYMLF) is now the third largest car company globally behind only Toyota (TM) and Volkswagen (VWAGY) at #1 and #2 respectively.

Culturally, Korean arts and film are gaining traction globally. Shows like Squid Game and movies like Parasite have gained worldwide acclaim in the past few years. And a quick browse through any social media app will show you the enormous popularity of K-pop.

And, since 1998, South Korea has had only one year of economic contraction. But that was in 2020 and not the fault of South Korean companies in the slightest. Even then, the country’s GDP only shrank by 0.7%. The Korean economy even grew 0.8% in 2009, at the height of the Global Financial Crisis.

Now, on to the ETF that gives you access to all of it.

The Money: I’m a Seoul Man

Unlike Poland, which only has one ETF pegged to its economy, the iShares MSCI Poland ETF (EPOL) that I have covered previously, with South Korea you have options. The Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF is one of three major ETFs that track the South Korean economy.

The largest is the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) which has a $16.6 billion in total assets, the smallest is the Matthews Korea Active ETF (MKOR) which has $100 million in total assets. FLKR sits at a nice middle ground between the other two at $465.8 million in total assets.

There is also the Direxion Daily MSCI South Korea Bull 3X ETF (KORU) which is pegged to the South Korean economy but is not a long-term buy and hold sort of investment like the other three. And, given that’s who I generally like to use ETFs, I will not be discussing it here.

Of the three major South Korea ETFs, FLKR is far and away the cheapest to hold with an expense ratio of 0.09% compared to EWY at 0.59% and MKOR at 0.79%. They all invest primarily in companies on the KOSPI Composite Index so their returns day-to-day are more or less the same.

In fact, I wrote this the day before a ceasefire was announced in the war between Iran, the U.S., and Israel. At that time, EWY, FLKR, and MKOR were down 2.65%, 2.35%, and 2.53% respectively (for a reason I will get to in the risks section of this article.) Over the past 12 months they are up 143%, 129%, and 115% respectively. They all also have basically the same top holdings.

FLKR’s top three are SK Hynix at 19.6%, Samsung at 18.89%, and Hyundai motor at 2.91%. Everything else in its portfolio is at 2.4% or less with 156 total holdings.

EWY’s top three holdings are Samsung at 23.8%, SK Hynix at 19%, and Hyundai at 2.59% with the other 83 holdings being 2.24% or less of its portfolio.

MKOR holds Samsung as its top holding at 19.9% with Samsung preferred shares making up another 4.8% and SK Hynix at 4.5% and all the rest of its 49 holdings sitting at less than 4.5%.

And for both FLKR and its competitors it has been SK Hynix and Samsung driving their growth over the past year. The AI-driven memory shortage, which I will discuss later in this article, has caused both companies to surge over the past 12 months.

Samsung is up about 60% since last April while SK Hynix is up over 500%. As far as I can tell, it's the fact that FLKR's top holding is SK Hynix that has caused it to outperform the other two. Speaking of...

In terms of total return in the long-term, it evens out as well. EWY is the oldest of the three, incepted in May of 2000. Since inception, it has managed an average return of 7.71% annually. MKOR is the second-oldest, incepted in October of 2010 and has averaged 8.55% annually. And FLKR is the youngest, incepted in November of 2018 and has averaged 11.13% annually.

The main reason for the difference in long-term return as far as I can tell is that EWY has the down years during the DotCom crash and the Global Financial Crisis weighing it down and MKOR just generally underperforms the other two.

The 1-year returns of the three for 2025 are MKOR at 96.7%, EWY at 97.57%, and FLKR at 173.71%. FLKR also handily outperforms the 3 and 5-year returns of the other two as well, with 39.84% and 13.21% for 3 and 5-year average returns respectively to 23.05% and 4.77% for EWY and 22.32% and 6.63% for MKOR.

I’ll get into why all three ETFs have been going gangbusters in the past year in the catalyst section of this article.

For now, the last things I want to touch on are the dividends paid by these ETFs and their liquidity.

FLKR wins hands down for yield with a TTM dividend yield of 3.11% to EWY’s 1.66% and MKOR’s 2%. FLKR has paid a dividend since 2017 and has only cut it twice in 2020 and 2023.

EWY has a longer history of paying a dividend but cuts it regularly with cuts in 2018, 2020, 2022, and 2024 in the last decade.

Finally, MKOR has only paid a dividend since 2024 and cut it in 2025.

FLKR is the middle ground in terms of liquidity with an average 3-month volume of 474,721. EWY is far and away the most widely-traded with a 3-month average volume of 20.5 million while MKOR is much less liquid with a 3-month average volume of 25,811.

While EWY is much more liquid, you shouldn’t have any trouble getting into or out of a position in FLKR and the greater liquidity does not make up for the dramatically higher expense ratio. As far as a projection for this year goes though, it gets somewhat complicated.

In the bull-case I see FLKR easily managing a 100% return. Even with all the chaos in Iran (which impacts South Korea in particular) the ETF is still up 25% this year. That would put us at $64.10 per share by the end of this year based on the $32 price FLKR started 2026 at.

In an even more extreme bull case where FLKR repeats last-year’s performance of 173% then share prices could hit $87.40 from the $32 it started the year at. However, I am significantly more comfortable with the $64 projection.

The bear case is dependent on conditions well outside of South Korea’s control and I couldn’t tell you exactly how far it could dip, but if the Iran situation continues for months or even years it would be disastrous for South Korea and, by extension, FLKR.

I would expect similar results for both EWY and MKOR in both the bull and the bear case but, altogether, FLKR is dramatically cheaper than its competitors, it pays a higher-yielding dividend, and it’s plenty liquid for your average investor’s purposes.

By my math, that makes it a better deal than its competitors, especially considering its out-performance compared to both EWY and MKOR over the past year.

However, the Iran situation brings me nicely to the main risk to FLKR…

The Risk: The Hormuz Crisis

Who knows what will happen with the current war between the United States, Israel, and Iran? I honestly have no idea and anyone claiming they know what will happen with the situation probably has something to sell you.

As of the evening of April 7, the Strait of Hormuz has been reopened after about a month of being closed. The United States and Iran have agreed to a two-week ceasefire, which is fantastic news but the situation remains fragile to say the very least.

Whether this is simply a pause in the conflict or the beginning of peace negotiations is still unclear. Either way, we're not out of the woods yet and that remains an enormous problem for South Korea and for this ETF.

See, about 20% or one fifth of all the world’s energy resources pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The strait is even narrower in reality than it looks on a map. The Strait is rather shallow so only a narrow portion of it is actually usable for ships.

After the war began, Iran closed the strait to all ships attempting to pass through it and the closure of Hormuz remains both Tehran’s trump card in any conflict with the U.S. and Israel and its most powerful bargaining chip in any peace negotiations.

While the U.S. was relatively unaffected by the closure, several American allies and a significant share of the global economy are dependent upon oil and natural gas from the Persian Gulf. Namely, Japan imports 57% of its energy through the Straits of Hormuz, India imports 50%, and South Korea imports 55%.

Now, if the ceasefire holds and the war ends soon, energy exports will begin flowing through the Strait of Hormuz again and everything returns to normal. The South Korean economic engine will continue purring along like it has done for the past several decades.

However, if peace negotiations don't pan out or the ceasefire falls apart and the war drags on, energy prices around the world will spike. And the most dramatic increases will be coming for South Korea and broader east and southeast Asia.

That would grind the economies of South Korea, Japan, and India in particular to a halt and would obviously cause a crash in their stock markets and the ETFs tracking them like FLKR.

It’s worth noting that Korea is working to lessen its dependence on Gulf oil and gas through nuclear energy. The country already generates about a third of its power from its 26 nuclear reactors and it plans to bring two new large reactors online by 2038.

But as you might have guessed from the 2038 goal for those two new reactors, nuclear is a long-term solution. It will do nothing to help in the short term if South Korea is cut off from the oil and gas it imports from the Persian Gulf.

So, that’s the risk here, but if you can stomach that risk, the KOSPI being down over the past few weeks and FLKR’s share price by extension creates a great buying opportunity for a long-term play.

FLKR was trading for just over $49 on February 27, just before the war started. It has been holding around $40 since with brief spikes above or below that figure. The ceasefire news caused the ETF to go back up to $44 as I write this but it's still off the near-$50 high it had earlier this year.

The war aside though, in the long-term, I am extremely bullish on South Korea because of the memory shortage and South Korea’s place as a major player in the AI hardware market. That brings me to…

The Catalyst: I Forgot to Remember to Forget

One of the biggest stories in tech this year has been the memory shortage. Artificial intelligence is hungry for more of everything, more power, more data centers, more processors, and more memory.

Random access memory or RAM and dynamic random access memory, DRAM, are in incredibly high demand. It has created a shortage of the hardware that Intel (INTC) CEO Lip-Bu Tan believes won’t be relieved until 2028 at least.

Alphabet (GOOG), Google’s parent company, might have mitigated the problem somewhat with its new TurboQuant technology. TurboQuant shrinks the amount of memory needed by AI by about six times.

However, even with that helping out, we do not make enough memory hardware at present for the needs of AI and it will take time to scale up the amount of it we can produce as there are only three companies that dominate the global memory market.

Of the three, two of them are Korean, SK Hynix and Samsung. The only non-Korean company is America’s Micron Technology (MU).

I’m sure you noticed earlier that Samsung and SK Hynix are the top two holdings in all three South Korea ETFs. SK Hynix and Samsung together make up about 40% of FLKR's portfolio and have driven its incredible growth over the past year.

The memory shortage is the reason why all three ETFs were either close to or exceeded a 100% gain in 2025. And SK Hynix’s chairman Chey Tae-won believes the memory shortage will last until 2030.

Regardless of how long it will go on for, the memory shortage is already paying off big-time for memory producers like Samsung which, last Tuesday, projected its January to March operating profit would come in at $37.8 billion which is an eightfold increase over the same period last year. Samsung is also projecting Q1 2026 revenue to be up 70% over Q1 2025, all driven by AI’s memory needs.

Even with Google’s new technology, the demand for memory far outstrips supply and it’s likely to remain that way for a couple of years at least or maybe until the end of the decade.

That does feed back into the risk though. Memory hardware production, like most computer hardware production, requires a lot of electricity. And if South Korea faces an energy crisis due to conditions in the Middle East, this catalyst is much less likely to materialize.

The Verdict: A Buy, if You Can Manage the Risk

All-in-all, provided the Strait of Hormuz is reopened and South Korea’s energy imports are able to continue, the South Korean economic miracle that began in the 1960s is likely to continue into the foreseeable future driven by the memory shortage.

And the Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF is the cheapest, and by my math the best, way to play that trend. Right now, we have a decent buying opportunity with the Hormuz crisis dragging the South Korean market and the ETFs invested in it down artificially.

However, even with the Strait of Hormuz reopened, the situation on the ground between the United States, Israel, and Iran remains rather fragile even with the two-week ceasefire. If the ceasefire breaks down and/or this becomes a protracted conflict it’s likely this is less of a buying opportunity and more the beginning of a long-term economic crisis in South Korea and Asia more broadly.

Right now, it looks like President Trump wants to wrap this war up in short order. But I’m not the only one having trouble pinning down exactly what Washington plans to do. Iran also likely doesn’t want this to drag on forever for obvious reasons.

That's probably why both sides agreed to the ceasefire, but now comes the arguably more difficult prospect of negotiating a lasting peace in the region. And, to quote Machiavelli, “Wars begin when you will, but they do not end when you please.”

So, FLKR is a buy from me with the significant caveat that the situation in the Middle East presents a major risk to it and to the South Korean economy.

data.title: TUR: Turkish Equities Set To Benefit If Iran Ceasefire Holds
data.description:
Introduction

Global equities are reacting positively to a ceasefire in Iran. While doubts naturally remain as to the durability of the ceasefire and the possibility of renewed fighting in the region, hopes are that the current ceasefire morphs into a lasting peace settlement between warring sides.

One key beneficiary of a prolonged peace in Iran will be Türkiye, which shares a land border with the Islamic Republic and has been one of the Islamic countries (along with Pakistan, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia) pushing for a ceasefire.

Investors interested in gaining exposure to Turkish equities may want to consider the iShares MSCI Turkey ETF (TUR) which I rank with a Buy rating. The bullish investment thesis for TUR can be summarized as:

  • Turkish stocks trade at an undemanding 14.1x trailing P/E multiple, notably cheaper than the S&P 500.
  • The 2.11% dividend is well-covered even after factoring in the elevated 0.59% expense ratio, with the 0.38x payout ratio leaving ample retained earnings.
  • Turkish GDP growth is projected to exceed that of the United States by 2030, likely resulting in stronger EPS growth for TUR relative to the S&P 500.

ETF Strategy and Characteristics

TUR is a passive ETF investing exclusively in Turkish equities traded on the Istanbul Stock Exchange (its benchmark is the MSCI Turkey IMI 25/50 index). As such, investors in TUR should be comfortable with currency risk which is arguably one of the main concerns for international investors, given Türkiye's unorthodox monetary policy and significant currency losses of the Turkish Lira (USD:TRY) versus the U.S. dollar in recent years.

Indeed from the snippet below you will see that a U.S. dollar was worth marginally less than 3 TRY a decade ago, increasing to about 44.5 TRY in April 2026.

Currency considerations aside, TUR's portfolio currently covers 78 individual holdings, with a top ten holdings concentration of about 51%. TUR is heavily invested in cyclical stocks from the industrials, financials, materials, consumer discretionary, and energy sectors, with these five sectors accounting for roughly 74% of ETF assets. Interestingly, the ETF has no information technology exposure, hence is a solid pick for investors worried about AI valuations.

As with most country-specific ETFs, the expense ratio is rather high at 0.59%, which is not a major concern over the short term, but certainly represents a drag on returns if you hold TUR for extended periods of time. However this is more than compensated by the attractive valuations offered by Turkish equities, as I will discuss in the next section.

Current Valuation

Even after the positive reaction to the ceasefire in Iran, I calculate TUR holdings trade at ~14.1x their trailing earnings (earnings yield of about 7.1%). This remains notably cheaper than the S&P 500, which offers a trailing P/E of about 27x (earnings yield of about 3.70%). In essence, current pricing suggests that S&P 500 earnings will grow roughly 3.4% faster in perpetuity, something I see as unlikely over the long term.

Of course I should note that in the near term the S&P 500 is widely expected to benefit from AI tailwinds, in contrast to TUR which has no exposure to the information technology sector. That said, as AI adoption grows across the economy, this benefit for large cap U.S. stocks should arguably fade. This is reflected in a slower EPS growth outlook for the S&P 500 of 10% in 2027 (versus 12% in 2026).

At the same time, prior to the Middle East war, Türkiye's economy was forecast to grow by 4.2% in real terms in 2026, roughly double the pace of GDP expansion in the United States. Despite the Iran ceasefire, I do expect the growth outlook to be revised down, albeit only marginally.

To sum up, I believe TUR offers attractive valuations relative to the S&P 500, with EPS growth driven by strong momentum in the Turkish economy.

Return Outlook

TUR offers an attractive 2.11% dividend yield, although growth over the past decade has been quite poor at negative 2.88%, impacted by the aforementioned currency headwinds. Factoring in the 0.59% expense ratio, I calculate a payout ratio for TUR of about 0.38x, indicating reasonable dividend safety despite the ETF's high allocation to cyclical sectors.

I arrive at the 0.38x payout ratio by using the 2.11% dividend yield, adding the 0.59% expense ratio, and dividing the sum by the earnings yield (7.1%) TUR trades at. In numbers, we get

2.11%+0.59%=2.7% dividend and expenses

2.7%/7.1%=0.38x payout ratio

I think adding the expense ratio is pertinent as it shows TUR's ability to pay out dividends after accounting for the expenses the ETF incurs. I opted to use the earnings yield in the calculation since it is regularly updated on the iShares website, akin to the dividend yield. In essence, investors are buying a portfolio of stocks which has produced an earnings yield of 7.10% over the past twelve months, and this portfolio of stocks supports a 2.7% dividend distribution after expenses.

With such a conservative payout ratio, we see that TUR's retention ratio is quite high at about 0.62x, indicating substantial benefits from retained earnings. While I believe the return on invested capital will be in excess of the 7.1% earnings yield TUR holdings trade at, I conservatively use the earnings yield to estimate a contribution of about 4.4%. In numbers, the calculation is as follows:

0.62*7.1%=4.4% contribution from retained earnings

The final contributor to TUR's earnings growth is set to come from economy-wide GDP expansion, which is likely to exceed that of the United States over the medium term. I will use the IMF's real GDP growth estimate of about 3.7% until 2030, adding 2% for inflation to arrive at a nominal benefit of 5.7%. Of course inflation in Türkiye is likely to be much higher than 2%, but that will be reflected in a weaker TRY exchange rate, hence is not relevant for U.S. dollar returns. My calculations are summarized in the table below:

Return Component Contribution Commentary
Dividend 2.11% Dividend appears well-covered thanks to a low 0.38x payout ratio, notwithstanding high cyclical sector exposure
Retained Earnings 4.4% Sizable benefit thanks to high retained earnings and low valuations
Nominal U.S. dollar GDP growth 5.7% Quite high thanks to a favorable economic outlook for the Turkish economy
Total 12.21% Overall attractive relative to the S&P 500

Source: Author calculations

As you can tell from the table above, I estimate TUR should deliver a U.S. dollar return of about 12.21% over the long term, of which only 17% is driven by current dividend income, with the remaining 83% dependent on capital gains. I believe this is quite attractive relative to the S&P 500, ranking TUR with a Buy rating.

Risks

In the near term, a collapse in the Iran ceasefire is a key risk in the investment case. Indeed persistent geopolitical tensions will likely weigh on economic sentiment in Türkiye, while economic hardship in Iran may actually result in a migrant wave, putting pressure on the economy of its Northern neighbor.

With 74% of ETF assets allocated to cyclical sectors (principally industrials and financials) weaker-than-expected GDP growth in Türkiye may result in EPS growth underperforming expectations. In any case, I expect TUR to remain volatile throughout the economic cycle, making the ETF only appropriate for investors comfortable with near-term losses during recessions.

Political risk is also a major consideration when investing in the country. The mayor of the largest city, Istanbul, has been thrown in jail, as he was seen as a potential candidate in the 2028 presidential elections. As such, excessive concentration of power in President Erdogan could reasonably be expected to spill over into poor corporate governance of Turkish companies, resulting in lackluster returns for investors.

Last but not least, while my total return outlook of about 12.21% is arguably achievable in U.S. dollar terms, higher-than-expected depreciation of the Turkish Lira could ultimately weigh on USD returns. As such, currency risk should always be top of mind for investors.

Conclusion

As I write this, Turkish equities are surging on the back of a ceasefire in Iran. While geopolitical risks will likely remain elevated in the region, I believe TUR will be one of the principal ETF beneficiaries if we see a sustained de-escalation in the Middle East war.

I estimate TUR can reasonably deliver a circa 12.21% total return, principally driven by EPS growth thanks to high retained earnings and robust GDP expansion in Türkiye.

For investors comfortable with the numerous risks that come with investing in the country, I believe TUR offers an attractive investment opportunity, ranking it with a Buy rating.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Transfer: 7.1% Yield And Potential Export Tailwinds Support Buy Case
data.description:
Energy Transfer (ET) has gained 15.7% since my last report largely, but not entirely closing the value gap I saw for the stock price. The increase in the stock price is not just driven by the situation in the Middle East since the stock price already started increasing in early 2026. In this report, I discuss whether the stock remains a buy or whether it's a good time to reduce the rating.

Energy Transfer Could Have Opportunities On Oil & Gas Export Shifts

Like many midstream companies, Energy Transfer has a business model that largely shields the company from fluctuations in commodity prices. Only 10% of the business is exposed to fluctuations in oil and gas prices which in the near term may provide an opportunity for Energy Transfer as commodity prices have risen sharply. The remaining 90% is fee based. Those fee-based contracts are take-or-pay which provides income regardless of whether capacity is utilized by the customer or not. They can also be volume based where customers pay based on the volumes flowing through the pipelines. With supply from the Middle East choked, there could be increased demand for North American oil and gas, which may provide a lift to the volumes flowing through the pipelines. There also is a potential risk as a slowdown in economic growth may reduce demand and subsequently pressure the volume side of the business.

In 2025, the company has around 40% of its EBITDA derived from natural gas related activities, 26% from liquid natural gas and refined products and 18% from crude oil.

Energy Transfer Is A Master Limited Partnership: What Does It Mean?

ET is a Master Limited Partnership or MLP. So, you buy common units of Energy Transfer instead of shares. MLPs are not statutorily required to distribute all cash that is not needed for operations and maintenance assets to their unit holders, but we do see generally there is some pressure to keep growing unit holder returns in the form of a cash distribution rather than a dividend. Since partnerships are generally not subject to federal or state income tax, everything flows through to the unitholders, who are required to report their share of the partnership’s reportable tax items.

Energy Transfer Stock Remains A Buy

Looking at the revision compared to prior to the war, we see that there is a modest 1% revision for revenues when summing up the quarterly analyst estimates and a 1.3% reduction in EPS. When looking at the annual estimates, we see a 3% revenue increase for this year along with a 0.7% increase to the EPS estimates, 4% for next year along with a 0.6% lift and 4.9% for 2028, but with a 9.5% reduction in the EPS estimate.

So, we see that estimates have not dramatically changed and that may seem boring but it also is the reason why many investors like midstream companies: The relative stability of the business. It also means that any increased production from U.S. companies for export purposes is not factored in.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Energy Transfer is set to grow sales 8.6% annually through 2028 with 5.8% growth in EBITDA, which is not great but acceptable. Free cash flow is expected to grow 7.4% annually which is much better. Even without any major upside from higher volumes or applying premium to the EV/EBITDA multiple, we note that the stock still has at least 20% upside to $22.67 and even 31% upside with a $24.86 target when shifting the scope to 2027.

Margins are expected to be lower this year. Free cash flow and operating cash flow are not expected to show a consistent upward trajectory either, but we note that the capital intensity will be lower this year and in the years ahead compared to 2025. Against the performance in 2023-2024, the capital intensity remains elevated. The net debt leverage remains in an acceptable range with yield on cost expected to rise to 7.8%. The current yield is 7.1%, which is lower than the 8.1% we previously saw but I do believe that the yield remains compelling.

Conclusion: Energy Transfer Remains Attractive

I believe that Energy Transfer remains an attractive stock to buy, especially for long-term oriented investors looking at safe income growth opportunities. Energy Transfer has additional upside, even when we are not applying any major boost from a possible increase in production volumes from oil and gas companies or a boost to the EV/EBITDA multiple. In that regard, the company has a somewhat safer upside compared to Kinder Morgan which needs a boost to the EV/EBITDA to show similar upside.

data.title: Roblox: I Like The Story, But I'm Staying On The Sidelines For Now (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s been practically a year since my last coverage of Roblox (RBLX), and my rating was a sell. Since then, RBLX stock has fallen 33%, and that made me a little more optimistic about the stock. Basically, my opinion was that there were some uncertainties but mainly a valuation that priced in perfection, but that the growth story was still appealing.

Now, we have a growth story that is still appealing, but the valuation is much more reasonable, considering that the company lost practically a third of its market cap in this period.

But even so, I don't think it's possible to be super optimistic about Roblox. The scenario clearly got better, and that’s why I’m giving an upgrade to a hold rating, but I still don't see that shareholder value generation is obvious for the coming years.

RBLX’s Growth Story Is Stronger

In the last article, Roblox's KPIs (key performance indicators) were already very interesting. The company had just reached ~21.7 billion hours engaged and was still showing a growth of 30%.

Even though only 3-4 quarters have passed, this indicator has already changed a lot. Now we see 35 billion hours engaged with a growth of 88% YoY, and the last quarter with this growth closer to 30% was precisely Q1, showing that the company accelerated this KPI a lot.

And other indicators also ended up following this good operational momentum: gross bookings reached $2.2 billion in the last quarter, revenue reached $1.4 billion, and the guidance for 2026 is still for a top line growth of around 25% YoY, which is still quite solid given the level reached.

But the best part of the case for me is not even that, but rather the narrative behind it. The company itself mentions so many positive points that could become a relevant source of revenue in the coming years, that it becomes a little easier to understand that there could be good and strong positive surprises in the next ~5-10 years.

Just to mention a few, at RDC, management mentioned some 5 year predictions for Roblox. Among them, I will highlight a few: A Fortune 500 company could use Roblox for the recruiting process; some schools could integrate a full k-12 curriculum with Roblox; thousands of adults could meet in “Roblox dating experiences”; and a Roblox dev could be valued at $1 billion.

Note that some are direct sources of revenue. School using Roblox? This could turn into something close to a SaaS. Recurring revenue, high total addressable market. Recruiting process? Same thing, recurring revenue with a TAM that is high and goes far beyond the Roblox “niche”. This is basically an endorsement that Roblox does not want to be just a gaming company, so the potential TAM is not x% of what the gamer community spends annually, but much more than that; it can capture a part of what schools spend, what private teachers spend, what recruiters spend, and what the government spends, perhaps (universities, public schools)?

And these initiatives for an older audience could also end up mitigating a relevant risk, which was the large user base growing up and migrating to other platforms, and some data, such as U.S. users between 18-34 years growing ~50% (more than under 18) ends up confirming that the execution of these initiatives is solid.

Roblox: Is $40 Billion Market Cap Cheap?

An analogy that I think can be made here is that the Roblox thesis is almost like YouTube in the beginning. It is based heavily on community and how users can experience different types of things. YouTube today is not just a streaming service; you use it for entertainment, but you can also use it for study, classes, podcasts, music, basically everything. In fact, just as Roblox is aiming for an RBLX dev to have a valuation of $1 billion, there are already YouTubers who have built a multi-million dollar business.

And with so many new revenue-generating initiatives, if Roblox truly manages to capture all of this—let's say, a few hundred million annually with school, a few billion annually with gaming, and a few hundred million with ads and other paid experiences—the $40 billion even seems low.

I mean, current revenue is at ~$1.4 billion per quarter. For 2026, revenue should be close to $6.1 billion, growing ~25%. If growth continues at this pace, we are talking about something close to $20 billion in 2033 (2x sales (P/S)).

It may seem like something very distant, but I don't think it's impossible. Doing some quick math, today there are just over ~100k schools in the US. If Roblox manages to extract $10,000/year from some type of monthly fee (which even seems cheap if you dilute it per student) from 100,000 schools worldwide, that is already an annual revenue increase of $1 billion, and that's from just one initiative. Note that I am talking about the whole world, so penetration would not need to be in 50% of US schools. It could be a penetration below 10% in several places in the world, and that math would already work.

I'm not saying schools are the big opportunity here. In fact, I'm saying exactly the opposite. This is just one of the initiatives that could be more mature in 10 years, and if 5 new markets already bring in ~$5 billion in revenue, the necessary growth in the core would have to be much smaller to reach the remaining $15 billion in 2033.

In fact, considering the guidance of ~$6.1 billion, the P/S for 2026 should be something close to 6.5x, which seems like a level that isn't very high but also far from being a bargain. For instance, great media/entertainment companies like Netflix (NFLX), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Meta (META), have a P/S higher than 7x. I know these are not exactly Roblox's peers, but as I said, Roblox could be more than just gaming, so I think it's fair to compare it with these behemoths just to see the "full potential". For great gaming companies, like Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) and Nintendo (NTDOY) (NTDOF), the P/S is about 5x. So 6.5x for RBLX does not seem so off.

But the attractiveness of RBLX’s valuation for me lies in asymmetry, in convexity. If growth remains at this level, and if several new markets are captured, in a 10-year period this multiple is compressed drastically, perhaps to 1x sales, or 0.5x sales, but obviously it depends heavily on execution and this momentum of its subniches.

Risks and Bottom Line

I like this characteristic of convexity in Roblox: numerous opportunities where some will materialize and greatly increase revenue, and others may flop.

But even so, I think $40 billion for so much uncertainty is a very high price to pay currently. That is, close to fair, but I don't think it's such a bargain as to warrant an upgrade to a buy.

One of the main uncertainties here is margin. In 2026 GAAP net income will likely still be very negative, and even looking at adj. EBITDA, we are talking about a margin of ~3%. So assuming that after 2030 Roblox reaches $20 billion in revenue, what will the margin be? With operating leverage, I think it could be 20%, or 30%, but that will require an almost flawless execution because the costs are not that low; there is the dev’s share, there are marketing costs, there are regulatory costs, there is a whole cost of CapEx and R&D that they are doing to reach this growth, and mainly, we are still seeing a stock-based compensation of ~$1.1 billion per year, and if that grows along with revenue, it means that even with an “appealing” adjusted EBITDA, the real shareholder value may not grow at the same pace as shareholder dilution (note in the chart below).

Besides this execution risk, I also see very strong regulatory risk here. In Brazil this subject is very hot. Recently there has been much discussion about a law (“ECA Digital” or “Digital Statute for Children and Adolescents”) that aims to protect children and adolescents digitally through “age checkers” for users, blocking some features such as chat for children on Roblox. This certainly affects the company. Perhaps it affects the demand from those under 18 for the platform depending on how features are reduced and consequently affects gross bookings.

In short, I still see some potential for Roblox, but the scenario is very cloudy. It will depend a lot on execution risk, and thinking about the next 5-10 years, a lot can get in the way of that, not only regulation but also competition, R&D costs, etc. Therefore, I upgrade from sell to hold, but still with this cautious stance.

data.title: AVNM: This Will Bounce Back If The Ceasefire Holds
data.description:
AVNM Overview

The Avantis All International Markets Equity ETF (AVNM) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund with AUM at ~$572MM that invests in global equities outside the U.S. Like AVGE, which I've covered previously, AVNM is structured as a fund-of-funds, meaning it achieves its mandate by investing entirely in other Avantis ETFs rather than buying individual stocks directly. Its benchmark target is the MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI, which covers large, mid, and small-cap exposure across developed and emerging markets outside the U.S.

For a fund only launched on June 27, 2023, the track record has been solid. In 2025, AVNM delivered a 38% return versus its benchmark's 32%, a gap of about 6%. While the recent geopolitical shock has seen the fund in the mid-$70s per unit, well off its 52-week high of $83, the fund is trading in line with its peers, and as an investor, if you want international equity, this is a solid fund that I rate a Buy on the heels of a ceasefire in Iran.

Fund Breakdown

AVNM was launched by Avantis (part of American Century Investments) to provide exposure to a diversified set of companies across international developed and emerging markets, with a specific focus on securities the managers believe have higher expected returns. As noted in the fund prospectus, the underlying holdings are a stack of other Avantis ETFs across international (both cap, geography, and industry). The target allocation is about 70% developed and 30% emerging markets, with some flexibility at the discretion of the management team. I've been critical of fund-of-funds structures in the past, including in my review of AVGE, where the kitchen-sink approach diluted returns. Here, though, the underlying funds are coherently assembled around a value-tilt thesis — there's clear logic to the allocation versus just throwing everything Avantis offers into a blender.

The expense ratio comes in at 0.31% net, which is essentially the same as the passive alternative ACWX (iShares MSCI ACWI ex US ETF) at 0.32%. That fee parity is a differentiator in AVNM's favor: if you're getting active management and a demonstrated track record of benchmark outperformance for the same cost as a passive alternative, the case for owning it starts to make a lot of sense. From a liquidity standpoint, the fund trades 77,000 shares per day, which is decent at ~$5MM but not deep. It's not a fund for large institutional block trades, but it's ok as a smaller investor.

The War Trade and Why It's a Buying Opportunity

Since the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran began in early March 2026 and the Strait of Hormuz was effectively shut to tanker traffic, AVNM has been hammered. Given the fund is entirely focused outside the U.S., with heavy exposure to developed markets in Europe and Asia — economies that are deeply reliant on Gulf oil flows to power their industrial bases. Japan, Germany, South Korea, and the UK, all meaningful weights in AVNM's underlying ETFs, import large volumes of Middle Eastern crude.

But if the ceasefire holds — and both parties agreed today - the case to buy AVNM at these levels is compelling. Asian and European equities have been oversold, and a reopening of Hormuz should unlock a meaningful re-rating. From $74, the fund has a runway back toward its prior highs.

Fund Portfolio and Risk Profile

The more nuanced point — and the one I think the market has missed — is that AVNM's sector composition actually sets it up well even if oil stays elevated. The fund holds a meaningfully higher weighting in materials and energy than its passive MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI benchmark. This was a drag during the 2025 AI-driven tech rally, where underweighting global tech hurt relative returns in the back half of the year, similar to the dynamic I identified with AVGE. But the current macro environment is different. Goldman Sachs has flagged that Brent crude could exceed $100/barrel, and even with a ceasefire, oil tanker insurance premiums and re-routing costs will keep freight rates elevated for months. That benefits international energy producers — many of whom are European and Canadian names that sit inside AVNM's underlying ETFs. The materials overweight similarly plays well into the infrastructure rebuild theme that Carney has championed in Canada and that European governments are pursuing aggressively under defense and energy security mandates.

Risks

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 0.88, which reflects meaningful lower volatility versus the broader market — though given the fund only has roughly 2.5 years of live data, I'd take this number with some caution. What I can say is that the qualitative setup — value-tilted, energy/materials overweight, broadly diversified across EM and developed markets — is actually a nicer configuration for what lies ahead than a passive benchmark that simply owns the market cap weight of global tech. The fund's Sharpe ratio is also close to 2, which is strong and impressive given its track record and macro volatility.

From a macro risk perspective, the key downside scenario is a ceasefire that doesn't hold and an escalation that further tightens the Hormuz chokepoint. That would continue to punish European and Asian industries and drag on AVNM. I'd also keep an eye on the CUSMA negotiations in July — Canadian equities are a component within some of the underlying Avantis ETFs, and any re-escalation in U.S.-Canada trade tensions could create additional headwinds for the fund's EM and small-cap buckets in particular.

Bottom Line

AVNM is a well-constructed, actively managed international equity ETF that has earned its fees so far with genuine outperformance versus its passive benchmark. The war-related sell-off has pushed the fund down from its highs and created what I believe is a good entry point for investors who want international equity exposure in an environment where the geopolitical overhang is starting to lift. At 0.31%, you're paying essentially the same fee as the passive ACWX alternative, and unlike ACWX, AVNM has consistently outperformed its benchmark. I rate AVNM a Buy contingent on ceasefire stability, as this is not the fund to hold if the conflict intensifies further. But for investors with a 12-to-18-month horizon who believe the worst of the Iran shock is behind us, AVNM is one of the more compelling risk-reward setups in the international equity space right now.

data.title: B&G Foods: Cheap For A Reason, But Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

B&G Foods, Inc. (BGS) is down over 80% over the past half a decade, as the company's business model was hit significantly by the high-rate environment, and with cheap money now gone, they were forced into a major dividend cut back in 2022 with fear of a new one looming once again alongside inflation, tariff uncertainty, and margin pressure.

However, the company is undergoing a significant transformation, aiming at reducing their size and leverage through significant portfolio adjustments, while the current valuation already accounts for very significant levels of risk.

Portfolio Turnaround Advances

B&G Foods reported an expectably weak 2025, marked by a significant ~$9.5 million hit from tariffs, cost pressures, significant impairment charges due to their ongoing portfolio transformation, which resulted in a drop in net sales, EPS, and virtually everything else. Meanwhile, the company also advanced on their debt repayment goals, with net interest expenses decreasing by 5% (~$7.8 million) to $149.6 million throughout the year.

The free cash flow reached $70.75 million in 2025, a steep decline from the $103.65 million in 2024 and $222.07 million in 2023 as a result of the weakness mentioned above combined with working capital changes and a normalization of artificial inflation in 2023 from the ~$137.8 million non-cash loss on sales of assets, while the drop from 2024 to 2025 can be explained by tariffs, higher CAPEX and downsizing overall. Note however that this also includes the $11.5 million deposit for their acquisitions, meaning that the normalized FCF would be closer to $82.25 million.

For FY26, BGS expects net sales to drop to $1.655 billion to $1.695 billion, with Adj. EBITDA relatively flat in the range of $265 million to $275 million and $0.55 to $0.65 in Adj. Diluted EPS.

The company’s portfolio transformation continues to advance, recently announcing the ~$110 million acquisition of the College Inn and Kitchen Basics brands, which are expected to generate between $110 million to $120 million in net sales, Adj. EBITDA of $18 million to $22 million Adj. Diluted EPS of $0.08 to $0.12, as per the company’s release.

This comes after recently announcing their Green Giant U.S. frozen business to Seneca Foods Corporation (SENEA) alongside their co-packing agreement, recently highlighting the pending divestiture of their Canadian Green Giant business, expecting their new portfolio to generate EBITDA growth, stronger cash flows, lower leverage and better margins with the company’s CEO stating the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

B&G Foods is making strong progress against our long-term goals, improving the base business net sales trends of the core business towards the long-term objective of plus 1%, reshaping the portfolio for future growth, stability, higher margins and cash flows, and finally, reducing leverage below 5.5x through divestitures and excess cash flow to facilitate strategic acquisitions.

Financially, based on BGS’ latest report, we see the company working on reducing their debt by selling assets, while the current assets continue to cover their current liabilities very well, although their leverage remains very elevated given how much money the company can make, and it’s no wonder that the current environment is pushing them into this kind of moves.

The Dividend Dilemma

BGS currently pays at a massive ~14.85% dividend yield, representing $60.6 million, which is covered by their cash flow despite the net profit being negative due to depreciation, amortization and major impairments, which are non-cash expenses that don’t affect their ability to pay the dividend.

However, this is far from a great payout ratio, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see a reduction here. In fact, I wouldn’t even mind it, as that can allow the company to focus on their turnaround instead of having to pay most of what they make back to shareholders. As a note, even if they cut it by a whopping 50% to free up ~$30 million in annual dividends that can help them accelerate debt reductions (~1.5% of the debt per year through this, which is not massive, but it can add up through interest savings) while still paying a very solid ~7.425% dividend and potentially attracting better financing yields.

This might not be up to them however, as if the turnaround doesn’t result in a relatively quick turnaround in cash flow, the refinancing of their 5.25% senior notes due in 2027 can easily close this gap. If we use the SOFR + 3.5% they have on their term loan B due in 2029, that’s roughly 7.16% today, meaning nearly $10 million in extra interest expenses, although adding back the tariff expenses and the deposit can offset even a higher rate. Note however that their 8% bonds currently trade at ~8.69%, and if the current macro pressure advances or the market’s confidence doesn’t improve, we could even see them refinance at even more than 9%.

Another aspect to note is the fact that we see the number of shares growing, but the proceeds from issuance of common stock remained at zero in 2025 and 2024. When seeing the numbers rise, we may be inclined to think that they issued these to cover the dividend or other costs, but this is strictly for their share-based compensation, not for funding something at these immense equity costs.

Near- & Long-Term Macro Uncertainty Grows

Deutsche Bank analyst Steve Powers and his team recently highlighted several factors that impact the CPG industry and can challenge the “defensive play” idea, as I’ve also shared before. They highlighted several trends affecting this industry in particular (i.e. besides factors such as tariffs, rate potential hikes, etc.), including the impact of growing GLP-1 adoption, private label brands, aging demographics, and the rising K-shaped economy, among others that I also described before.

Although it can be an extra point of pressure, I’ll continue to highlight that GLP-1 by itself will very likely not be as significant as the market believes. For example, Morgan Stanley recently increased their estimates and now expect about 15% of the US population to use GLP-1 medication by 2035.

What does that mean for food consumption? It depends on the study, household budgets, and the specific segment of the industry we’re looking at, but we can generally expect a hit of about 5.5% in grocery spending, with a low-double-digit impact on sweets/snacks. As a simple example, if we assume it’s a generous 10% drop in BGS’s products and 15% of the population will take the medicine by 2035, that’s basically a 1.5% drop in spending for BGS’s products, so basically a 1.5% drop in volume (constant) over a decade, which can easily be countered by price increases during this time, being far from a major issue.

Instead, factors such as private labels are probably going to have the biggest impact, in my opinion, as we’re talking about an industry that’s already extremely competitive, and having a better option from a value or even quality point of view can be detrimental for legacy brands. BGS and their peers constantly spend hundreds of millions or even billions to build and maintain their brands, and the current environment can push consumers into more trade-offs, with the K-shaped economy potentially being worsened by the risk of seeing elevated rates for longer.

It’s true that these are all risks and they can add up to significant pressure, but I believe it’s also important to note that these companies aren’t acting in their own bubble, and they are able to adapt to these trends, sell segments, cut brands, diversify, modernize products for trends, etc., potentially offering “a way out” despite increasing the risk since these will ultimately depend on their management’s ability to navigate this.

One thing is certain however - these are not the same “defensive” companies that investors once considered them, and GLP-1 is just the cherry on top of several fundamental problems that have been brewing for over a decade, and I believe they should be valued properly given these risks.

And everything above comes on top of the potential inflationary shock coming from the Iran conflict. I highlighted before that even if we see a complete stop to this conflict, it can still take months or even years to see oil and gas flows recover and the current war premium fade, which can be enough to push the Fed into pausing rate cuts for longer or even hiking, which would be terrible for BGS, for which every bit of savings on interest expenses matters significantly.

Valuation

For BGS’ DCF to Equity, starting from their $82.25 million adjusted FCF seen in 2025 (after the deposit) and assuming a normalization in tariffs, that alone gets us to a base of around $90 million now, but I’ll also assume -$50 million in net borrowings in 2026, 2027 and 2028 (each) to account for the company reducing their leverage, with a CAGR of 3% over the first 5 years to take into account for their portfolio transformation, potential macro improvements, debt reductions, slightly offset by their potentially higher interest expenses.

This would be followed by a 2.75% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, assuming a very high 15% discount rate to take into account the significant risk this comes with and demand a very high return, with 80.881 million shares outstanding as of the Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $620.60 million, for an intrinsic value of $7.67, which is above the current $5.12 at the moment of writing this, and that’s when already taking into account relatively conservative estimates, resulting in a solid margin of safety.

Conclusions

BGS is rated Buy, backed by significant portfolio transformation actions and an attractive valuation that already implies a high margin of safety, with a massive dividend even if they decide to cut it.

There are several risks tied to their turnaround and the industry as a whole, but the risk-reward seems to be in the company’s favor now, with potential to deliver solid returns.

data.title: 14-Day Ceasefire With Iran: Sell Oil And Buy Oil Companies
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to sell assets that track oil and buy assets that track oil companies. Fortunately, the Iran conflict did not escalate into a catastrophic situation, and a 14-day ceasefire was announced. My intention with this article is to share my perceptions on the topic and show the clearest investment thesis given this situation.

The Chronology of the Ceasefire

The situation in Iran was heading towards a tragic outcome as the deadline imposed by President Trump passed. The US attacked the island of Kharg in Iran, and President Trump even claimed that an entire civilization would die that night. Fortunately, the situation headed towards a positive outcome.

News reports indicate that Pakistan was responsible for mediating the situation. Initially, the country's authorities asked American authorities to increase the negotiation period to two weeks. In return, the Pakistani Prime Minister requested the Iranians to agree to reopen the Strait of Hormuz for two weeks.

President Trump published on Truth Social that he decided to postpone the attacks, and Iran's Supreme Security Council indicated that negotiations with the United States will begin on Friday, April 10, in Islamabad, after presenting a 10-point proposal.

Although this truce does not signal the end of the war, it is a great relief! The first ships have already started crossing the Strait of Hormuz, as we can see below. The stock market reacted positively to this truce.

Impact on the Stock Market

As a consequence of the ceasefire, the stock market, represented by Dow Futures, S&P Futures, and Nasdaq Futures, is expected to soar. At the same time, oil fell below $100 due to expectations of an increase in supply with the reopening of the Strait.

All the data mentioned above serves as a background to understand the current scenario. Now, I am able to provide my outlook for the future, such as which assets to buy and sell.

Which Assets to Buy?

I will remain consistent with my latest recommendations. On March 30th I published an article commenting on the investment thesis of selling oil and buying oil companies. In my opinion, this is the best trade at the moment.

Although the share price of oil companies may initially fall due to the reduction in tensions, there is a strong expectation that the gap between the performance of oil and oil companies will close, and this is explained by the price of oil being high for longer.

The conflict began at the end of February, and oil prices were already rising. The expectation for the next 14 days is relief combined with tension over the final result, so it is unlikely that oil will return to its price at the beginning of the year.

This situation generates volatility in the price of oil but has a positive effect on the cash flow of oil companies. The big point is that this cash flow will only appear in the quarterly results, and this is perhaps the biggest trigger for this thesis.

This trade already occurred last year with other commodities. One example is the price of gold, which preceded the rise of gold miners. I had the privilege of describing both theses and helping SA readers with their investment decisions.

Now applying this concept to the current scenario, since the beginning of the conflict, the United States Oil Fund LP (NYSEARCA:USO) ETF has risen 58%, while the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR (NYSEARCA:XLE) ETF has risen only 5.47%. My target for XLE is closing the gap to USO this year.

My investment thesis seems to make a lot of sense, as the post-ceasefire market opening indicates a much bigger drop for USO than XLE. Therefore, I reiterate the recommendation to sell assets that track the price of oil and buy oil companies, such as XLE or my top pick for 2026, the Brazilian Petrobras (NYSE:PBR).

Threats to Thesis

Although it is not the base scenario, the main risk for the thesis is a new escalation in the conflict. This would involve disagreements between Americans and Iranians and a re-escalation of the conflict after 14 days. Although this still benefits oil companies, the increased risk could bring down their prices.

The Bottom Line

After strong fears that the conflict in Iran would escalate into a catastrophic situation, the announcement of a 14-day ceasefire is a welcome relief. After analyzing the chronology of facts and market behavior, it is possible to outline some scenarios.

One of the clearest consequences of this prolonged conflict, in my opinion, is the improvement in the cash flow of oil companies due to higher oil prices for longer. This thesis is confirmed by the gap between the price of oil and the price of oil company shares.

Therefore, I reiterate the recommendation to sell assets that track the price of oil, like USO, and buy assets that track oil companies, like XLE. This is the most asymmetric thesis at the moment, in my opinion, and the price performance of both in the pre-market confirms this.

data.title: Astrana Health: A Delegated Risk Model At A Discount
data.description:
Thesis

Astrana Health, Inc.’s (ASTH) buy case for me really comes down to a mispriced growth compounder with a few structural advantages. We have a company delivering between 20% and 30% top and bottom line growth, strong free cash flow generation, and even clear operating leverage, yet if we glance over the valuation, it’s still trading at pretty deep discounts compared to peers. So it seems the market is essentially pricing in execution or regulatory risk.

The upside I see from this is that Astrana has a very diversified, risk-bearing model with strong cost control, as I'll explain. They also have an impressive G&A efficiency at around 6.8%, which would suggest it’s better insulated than most of the competition. I feel that if they simply execute on guidance and maintain margins, there’s meaningful multiple expansion potential to come in 2026 alongside earnings growth. It’s quite an attractive risk/reward setup.

It's also worth noting here that Astrana recently disclosed some material weakness in internal controls from those acquisitions. It likely contributes a lot to the current valuation discount and can't be ignored. It's more so from integration complexity rather than underlying business performance, in my opinion, and management expects no changes to reported financials, which was good to hear.

FY25 review

Just to take a quick look at their FY25 earnings, we did see some pretty significant growth across revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and even free cash flow. Total revenue came in at $3.18 billion, about a 56% increase from the $2.03 billion we saw last year. We can put this mainly down to their Care Partners segment, which grew about 55% to contribute a hefty $3.02 billion. There was also a nice increase in adjusted EBITDA to $205.4 million, up 21% from $170.4 million a year ago. So all in all, there was pretty strong operational leverage here, even with some elevated costs from acquisitions and integration activities.

In terms of net income, Astrana hit $22.5 million, with diluted earnings per share of around $0.46, which was modest profitability within a very highly capital-intensive growth environment. Free cash flow also more than doubled to about $104.5 million. What I thought was most notable here was that in Q4 '25 alone, revenue hit $950.5 million in revenue, a 43% jump year-over-year, with adjusted EBITDA coming in at $52.5 million, which was a 50% increase. So there is clearly strong momentum for the company going into 2026.

As for the balance sheet, we’re seeing the effects of the expansion efforts. Total assets jumped to $2.22 billion from $1.35 billion, with a good part of that coming from goodwill and intangible assets from acquisitions, which would include the accelerated integration of Prospect Health. On the liabilities side, their long-term debt did increase significantly to $991 million. It’s worth mentioning here that this is mainly from financing activity for the acquisitions and growth initiatives.

But despite higher leverage, they still managed to maintain decent cash management, with about $114.6 million of net cash coming from operating activities and a net increase in cash of $434 million. Elsewhere, the board increased the share repurchase program from $50 million to $100 million, which was nice to see. As for guidance, we can expect 24% revenue growth and about 29% in adjusted EBITDA growth at the midpoints. Total revenue is projected between $3.8 billion and $4.1 billion, and adjusted EBITDA is projected to fall somewhere between $250 million and $280 million. Management is clearly quite optimistic about continued scale benefits from their tech-enabled, risk-bearing care model.

Business model overview

When it comes to Astrana’s business model, things are quite different from traditional healthcare operators in the sense that their model is somewhat in a risk-bearing delegated payer layer, rather than just a simple provider or a pure insurer. What I mean by this is that instead of being paid per visit or procedure as a fee-for-service, Astrana receives a fixed and very valuable recurring stream of premium dollars from multiple insurance partners. They have the likes of Medicare, Medicaid and a commercial side in the stream and would also assume responsibility for managing the total cost of care for those patients. They then distribute those funds across their network of physicians/specialists whilst also handling the key payer-like functions such as claims processing, prior authorizations, and care coordination. So in that sense, they act as a single-payer-like operating structure across those fragmented insurers, which is what allows Astrana to actually unify incentives across patients and providers. Now, this is extremely important since it has typically been something the traditional system has struggled to achieve.

Just to expand on that more, what I see as making this model particularly resilient going forward is the alignment of the financial incentives with long-term patient outcomes. Overall, there is a lot of diversification embedded in the system as well. And because Astrana takes on full or partial risk for the total cost of care, they end up being economically incentivized to invest in preventive care, chronic disease management, and also early intervention. With the aim of reducing expensive downstream utilization like hospitalizations.

But on the flip side, they obviously have that multi-payer, multi-line structure spanning those populations like Medicare Advantage/Medicaid to help reduce dependence on any single reimbursement stream. That's what smooths earnings volatility for them here.

What's important is that this model also allows for what they call longitudinal patient engagement across insurance switches, in the sense that patients often stay within Astrana’s network even if they change plans. So they’re getting the added bonus of preserving data continuity. We also have to keep in mind that they combine this with their technology platform and their provider network. I see it as somewhat of a self-reinforcing flywheel in that better data and coordination lead to much lower costs and better outcomes.

Consistent growth track record

As you know, Astrana has shown us a pretty rare combination of high growth with disciplined cost control, in recent times, and going forward, this should be particularly favorable. Over the past 7 or so years, the company has scaled very aggressively. They managed to hit about 6x in growth in revenue and nearly 8x growth in free cash flow, whilst still delivering 20% to 30% compound annual growth rate. We also saw this momentum continue into 2025, in which Astrana hit a record $3.18 billion in revenue.

The good thing here is that this growth is not purely top-line driven, but it’s actually supported by improving efficiency and operating leverage. You see, the company has consistently kept its medical cost trend in the mid-single digits at the 4% to 5% level, which has outperformed a lot of their peers.

In terms of the competition, we usually see the main challenge as higher utilization and cost inflation. But on the administrative side, Astrana operates with a structurally much lower cost base. G&A was just 6.8% of revenue in 2025, compared to about 10% plus for traditional payers. They also managed to improve that ratio by about 75 basis points and 110 basis points on an adjusted basis year-over-year.

Now, I think we can put this efficiency down to their proprietary, internally built platform, as I explained earlier. The big advantage is that it automates large portions of the claims processing and prior authorizations, so it essentially allows them to scale without the proportional increase in overhead. As a result, the business is growing pretty rapidly but is also expanding margins over time, with the set-up now being to sustain 20% to 30% growth in both revenue and EBITDA into 2026.

Mixed membership outlook

Just to touch briefly on one area of concern, it would be the membership outlook that looks somewhat mixed. However, I still think it’s manageable within the context of the diversified model I outlined. On the positive side, we have the Medicare Advantage, which is the most attractive and stable segment, still showing us mid-single-digit growth and solid visibility. This should provide a reliable core earnings base.
